questions.question:
Read the statements about events A and B:
Statement I: P(A|B) is defined only when P(B) is non-zero.
Statement II: If A and B are independent, P(A|B) = P(A).
Statement III: P(A|B) is always equal to P(B|A).
For two events, P(A ∩ B) = 0.18 and P(B) = 0.30. Which value of P(A|B) follows directly?
A student says that two events are independent because they cannot occur together. Which correction is most accurate?
options: Mutually exclusive events with positive probabilities are not independent because occurrence of one rules out the other.
The statement is correct because no common outcome means no dependence.
Independence means P(A ∩ B) must be zero for all events.
Mutual exclusiveness and independence are the same condition written differently.

questions.question: Which option best captures why Bayes' theorem is useful?
options: It updates the probability of a cause after observing an effect.
It replaces conditional probability with simple addition of events.
It applies only when all events have equal probability.
It proves that P(A|B) and P(B|A) are identical.

questions.question: Assertion: If A and B are independent, then P(A ∩ B) = P(A)P(B). Reason: Independence means the occurrence of one event does not change the probability of the other.
options: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and the Reason explains the Assertion.
Both are true, but the Reason applies only to mutually exclusive events.
Assertion is true, but Reason reverses conditional probability.
Assertion is false, while Reason is true.
